author: @gen81465
text: I'll bet that many of the students I worked with back in college would have liked that program. Sadly, that was about 38 years ago, so they didn't even have USB drives. We worked with floppy disks. My first year in college (Autumn 1983), I was so far ahead of everyone else in the class that the instructor had me help run the computer lab for him. I remember some of the students taking hours to write a simple "Hello, World!" program, even in BASIC. To help them along, I came up with a short poem and put a copy of it on the wall of the computer room. It read "When your program looks complete, now press Alt Control Delete." For some strange reason, the instructor didn't seem to see a lot of humor in it, while I thought it was funny as hell.

author: @boblake2340
text: This only wotks if you do a restore immediatly after deletion. Wrtting of any files after detion of the original files will corrupt the others.

replies.author: @ZoVoSinc
replies.text: not necessarily true, especially with ssds or any flash based memory, to preserve memory life most memory chip storage devices will cycles the oldest blocks first. If you have say 64gb and have it 8gb filled, delete it and write a extra 32 there is a good chance it will have used other redundant space first to preserve the life of the memory chips and still be recoverable.

author: @stevetobias4890
text: Windows to Windows will work because of same file formats. This video enforces the good habits to get into, backup all files before you do anything if they mean anything to you. I still have backups of files from 10 years ago. I have a 2TB & 1TB HDD just for backups, files I really need get backed up to both drives so I have the original and two backups.

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: I'm the same I always have to have a backup just in case, because you never know. I'm glad I have done this because it was not that long ago my HDD corrupted and boom I still had all of my data! :)

author: @William_Hada
text: This is a great tool too have. Thanks for sharing! When you delete a file you are actually just turning on code that says don't read this. This program just turns the read switch back on. To wipe/erase a file completely you have to overwrite it with something else in that space.
